questions.question: Explain the differences between bandwidth and throughput. Under what conditions can throughput be lower than bandwidth?
keyPoints: Bandwidth is maximum theoretical data transfer capacity measured in bps
Throughput is actual amount of data successfully transferred in practice
Throughput < bandwidth due to: network congestion, packet loss, protocol overhead, hardware limitations, distance

questions.question: Describe the three main types of Area Networks with one example each and their typical coverage areas.
keyPoints: LAN: Local Area Network - building/campus, example: office WiFi, 1 km max
MAN: Metropolitan Area Network - city/campus, example: city cable network, 10-100 km
WAN: Wide Area Network - country/continent, example: Internet, unlimited range

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Star topology in computer networks?
keyPoints: Advantages: Easy to install/manage, fault detection easy, failure of one node doesn't affect others, scalable
Disadvantages: Central device failure brings entire network down, requires more cable than bus, performance depends on central hub capacity

questions.question: Differentiate between Peer-to-Peer and Client-Server network models with respect to scalability, security, and cost.
keyPoints: P2P: Simple, cheap for small networks (<10 nodes), no central management, less secure, hard to scale
Client-Server: Centralized resources, better security, scalable for large networks, requires dedicated server and admin, single point of failure

questions.question: List and explain the four components of network latency.
keyPoints: Propagation delay: Time for signal to travel through medium (distance/speed of signal)
Transmission delay: Time to push all packet bits onto the link (packet size/bandwidth)
Processing delay: Time for routers to examine and forward packet
Queuing delay: Time packet waits in router buffers before processing

questions.question: Compare Bus, Ring, and Mesh topologies on the basis of fault tolerance, cost, and scalability.
keyPoints: Bus: Low cost, moderate fault tolerance (one break affects whole), medium scalability
Ring: Moderate cost, single break can disable network, moderate scalability
Mesh: High cost (more cables), excellent fault tolerance (multiple paths), difficult to scale but reliable

questions.question: What are the three transmission modes in data communication? Explain each with a real-world example.
keyPoints: Simplex: One-way communication only - keyboard to computer, monitor display
Half-Duplex: Two-way but not simultaneous - walkie-talkie, CB radio
Full-Duplex: Simultaneous two-way - telephone, video conferencing

questions.question: A company needs to set up a network for 50 employees across 3 floors. Each floor has different departments with varying resource sharing requirements. Design an appropriate network topology and justify your choice considering factors like cost, fault tolerance, scalability, and manageability.
keyPoints: Recommend Hybrid topology: Star topology within each floor (for easy management), connected via Ring or Mesh backbone
Justification: Star provides easy fault isolation per floor, central switch per floor simplifies administration
Ring/Mesh backbone between floors provides redundancy if one floor's switch fails
Cost considerations: Star is cost-effective for LAN, backbone adds redundancy cost
Scalability: Easy to add nodes within floors, additional floors can connect to backbone
Management: Centralized switch per floor makes troubleshooting easier
Alternative acceptable: Full mesh for maximum fault tolerance but higher cost

questions.question: Explain the Nyquist Theorem and Shannon's Capacity theorem for channel capacity. A channel has a bandwidth of 4 kHz and signal-to-noise ratio of 30 dB. Calculate the maximum data rate using both theorems (assuming 2 signal levels for Nyquist).
keyPoints: Nyquist Theorem: Maximum data rate = 2B × log₂M where B=bandwidth, M=signal levels
For 2 signal levels (M=2): Max = 2 × 4000 × log₂2 = 8000 × 1 = 8 kbps
Shannon's Capacity: C = B × log₂(1 + S/N) where S/N is signal-to-noise ratio
Convert 30 dB to ratio: S/N = 10^(30/10) = 10^3 = 1000
Nyquist gives theoretical maximum for noiseless channel, Shannon accounts for noise
In practice, actual capacity limited by both theorems, Shannon provides more realistic limit

questions.question: Discuss the criteria for evaluating computer network performance. How does each criterion impact real-world network design decisions?
keyPoints: Performance: Measured by bandwidth (capacity), throughput (actual speed), latency (delay), response time. Impact: Determines user experience, requires appropriate hardware and topology selection
Reliability: Measured by frequency of failures, mean time between failures (MTBF). Impact: Critical applications need redundant paths, fault-tolerant topologies (mesh)
Security: Protection against unauthorized access, data integrity, availability. Impact: Determines encryption, access control, firewall placement decisions
Scalability: Ability to grow in size and functionality. Impact: Network design must accommodate future expansion, choose scalable topologies
Manageability: Ease of monitoring, troubleshooting, configuration. Impact: Centralized management tools, well-documented architecture
